exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
instructions: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
Lie supine on the incline bench with your head supported, feet flat on the floor, and back slightly arched while maintaining scapular retraction.
Grip the barbell with a pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your upper chest/shoulder line, then stabilize before starting the descent.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the upper chest/clavicular area, keeping elbows at roughly a 30–60° angle from the torso (avoid flaring to 90°).
Lightly touch or stop just above the chest, pause briefly without bouncing, then exhale and press the bar upward and slightly back to the start position, fully extending the elbows.
Keep core braced and feet planted throughout to maintain stability and prevent excessive lower back extension.
Carefully re-rack the bar under control or have a spotter assist when finished.
exercises.description: A barbell press performed on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest by pressing the bar upward and slightly toward the clavicle. It also engages the front delts and triceps for pressing and locking out the weight.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Fly
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered (split) stance or feet shoulder-width apart with a slight forward lean from the hips so you have stable balance; brace your core.
Grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip and take one step forward until you feel light tension in the cables; keep a small bend in the elbows throughout.
Start with your arms out to the sides roughly at shoulder height (or slightly higher for a high-cable fly) and chest lifted; retract your shoulder blades slightly to protect the joints.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a controlled arc in front of your chest at midline, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the finish.
Pause briefly with hands meeting or slightly overlapping, then inhale and slowly resist the cables as you return your arms to the start position using the same arcing path.
Keep the movement smooth and avoid locking the elbows, excessive torso rotation, or shrugging the shoulders; maintain core tension for stability.
exercises.description: Standing cable fly performed with the pulleys set high to mid-high engages the chest by bringing the arms together in a wide arc; the shoulders and core stabilize the movement. It emphasizes the pectoral fibers with controlled concentric adduction and slow eccentric return.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered or shoulder-width stance with knees soft and core braced (slight forward lean at the hips).
Reach down and grab each handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip, palms facing in or up, elbows slightly bent and fixed in that position throughout the movement.
Begin with the arms out to the sides and low (handles near hips or knees) and inhale.
Exhale and pull the handles upward and inward in a wide arc, keeping the elbows slightly bent, bringing the hands together or in front of the chest/upper sternum.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze the pectorals while avoiding excessive shoulder shrugging.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, allowing the cables to return the arms to the start position while maintaining tension in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions and re-rack safely; keep torso stable and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly is a low-to-high cable chest fly that targets the pectorals by sweeping the arms upward and together from low pulley positions; performed standing with a slight forward lean and a soft bend in the elbows to emphasize the chest contraction.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level when seated and your back rests flat against the pad.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level when seated and your back rests flat against the pad.
Select an appropriate weight and sit upright with feet flat on the floor, chest up and shoulders down and back.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and position your upper arms roughly parallel to the floor with a small bend in the elbows.
Start with the handles out to the sides so your chest is stretched but shoulders are not overstretched; maintain a stable torso against the back pad.
Exhale and drive the handles together in a smooth horizontal arc by contracting the pectorals, bringing your hands close together or slightly in front of the chest.
Pause briefly at peak contraction, squeezing the chest while keeping elbows slightly bent and avoiding shoulder shrugging.
Inhale and slowly reverse the movement under control, allowing the arms to return to the start position until you feel a controlled stretch in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding leaning forward or using momentum to move the weight.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly that brings the arms together in a horizontal arc to isolate and contract the pectoral muscles. Performed on a lever/pec-deck machine with the chest supported and elbows slightly bent throughout the movement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Svend Press
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position).
Hold one dumbbell vertically at chest level with both hands around the handle/ends, palms facing each other and elbows pointing slightly downward and tucked toward the body.
Squeeze the dumbbell firmly between your hands to engage the chest and forearm grip before initiating the press.
Exhale and press the dumbbell straight forward away from your chest, extending the arms until they are nearly straight but without locking the elbows.
Keep the shoulders down and stable (avoid shrugging) and maintain the squeeze on the weight to maximize pectoral tension throughout the movement.
Pause briefly at full extension, keeping the core tight and spine neutral.
Inhale and slowly return the dumbbell back to the starting position at the chest under control, stopping when the elbows are slightly bent.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and constant chest engagement; if balance or form breaks, reduce weight.
exercises.description: A standing pressing movement where a single dumbbell is squeezed and pressed straight out from the chest to create constant anterior chest tension. It emphasizes medial chest contraction while requiring shoulder and triceps stabilization and core bracing.

exercises.name: Barbell Feet Flat Bench Press
instructions: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
Lie supine on the flat bench with your eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and planted for stability, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (pinch them together and press them into the bench) to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the bar with a full grip slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over forearms and elbows under the bar.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar into the start position directly above your shoulders.
Inhale, then lower the bar in a controlled manner to mid-chest / nipple line, keeping elbows at about a 45° angle to the torso and maintaining scapular retraction.
Briefly pause with the bar lightly contacting the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar upward along a slightly arced path back to full arm extension.
Lock out the elbows at the top without overextending, keep the feet planted and core braced throughout the set.
After the final rep, carefully rack the bar onto the supports or have your spotter assist; use a spotter for heavy loads and maintain strict control on every rep.
exercises.description: Barbell feet-flat flat bench press is a supine pressing exercise that primarily works the pectorals by pressing a loaded barbell from the chest to full arm extension. Keeping the feet flat provides a stable base while the triceps and delts assist in elbow extension and shoulder stabilization.

exercises.name: Smith Bench Press
instructions: Set the Smith machine bar to an appropriate height, add desired weight, and set safety stops slightly below chest level.
Position a flat bench in the machine so the bar will line up with the mid-chest when you are lying down.
Lie supine on the bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and a slight natural arch in the low back; retract your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grasp the bar with a medium grip (hands slightly wider than shoulder-width) and ensure wrists are stacked over elbows; keep head on the bench.
Unrack the bar by rotating it or disengaging the safety, then lower the bar under control to the mid-chest/sternum while keeping elbows at about a 30–60° angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar lightly touching the chest (do not bounce), then press the bar up explosively until arms are nearly straight without locking the elbows aggressively.
Maintain a tight core and pressed scapulae throughout the movement for stability; avoid excessive flaring of the elbows or hyperextending the lower back.
Control the descent on each rep and re-rack the bar by rotating it back into the safety catches when finished.
exercises.description: A Smith machine variation of the flat bench press performed lying supine on a flat bench, pressing the fixed bar vertically to work the chest. The guided bar path reduces stabilization demand while emphasizing pressing strength in the pectorals.

exercises.name: Lever Crossovers
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest level when you sit upright.
Sit with your back pressed firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees at about 90°.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-down grip and start with your arms opened out to the sides at shoulder height, elbows slightly bent.
Brace your core and keep your torso stable; avoid leaning forward or arching the lower back.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a slow, controlled arc in front of your chest, allowing the hands to cross slightly if the machine path allows.
Pause briefly at full contraction and focus on squeezing the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position, maintaining the slight bend in the elbows and control throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping tension on the chest and avoiding momentum.
exercises.description: Seated lever crossovers are a machine chest fly variation that brings the arms together in front of the body to isolate and load the pectorals while minimizing torso movement. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and a controlled arc to squeeze the chest at peak contraction.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Pullover
instructions: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet under the pads so your body is stable and supine on the bench.
Grasp a barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip about shoulder-width apart and lift it straight over your chest with elbows slightly bent.
Brace your core and keep your hips down to maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc behind your head by hinging at the shoulders; keep a slight bend in the elbows and do not let them flare excessively.
Lower until you feel a deep stretch in the chest/lats or until the bar is roughly level with the crown of your head — do not go so far that your lower back overarches.
Exhale and pull the barbell back in the same arc to the start position over your chest by contracting the pectorals and lats; keep the movement smooth and controlled.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining steady tempo and full control on both lowering and lifting phases.
Use a spotter or lighter weight if you cannot control the bar safely behind your head; stop if you feel sharp shoulder or back pain.
exercises.description: Lying supine on a decline bench, the barbell pullover moves the weight in an arc from over the chest to behind the head, emphasizing a deep stretch and contraction of the chest while also engaging the lats and triceps.

tips: Focus on form
Control the eccentric phase

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent ~90°, palms facing forward or slightly inward and wrists neutral.
Inhale, keep the spine neutral and shoulders down away from the ears.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead (avoid locking the elbows).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and keeping the torso stable.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height with control to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Use a lighter weight or a spotter if form breaks down or if pressing heavy loads.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press is a compound pressing movement performed from a seated, back‑supported position that primarily develops the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper back for stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Reverse Butterfly
exercises.notes: Stand hip-width with a slight bend in the knees and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
instructions: Stand hip-width with a slight bend in the knees and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).

Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is tilted forward (about 30–45 degrees), keeping the spine neutral and neck long; let the dumbbells hang in front of your chest with elbows bent ~90° and dumbbells close together.
Brace your abs and maintain a stable torso; do not rotate or arch your lower back during the movement.

Exhale and open your elbows out to the sides by squeezing your rear delts and pulling your shoulder blades together, moving the dumbbells outward and slightly back until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or in line with your torso.
Pause briefly at the top and feel the contraction between the shoulder blades.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control, keeping the elbow bend and avoiding momentum.
Avoid shrugging (do not elevate the shoulders) and keep the motion driven by the posterior delts and upper back rather than the forearms or lower back.
exercises.description: A standing, bent-over reverse fly variation performed with dumbbells to target the rear delts and upper back by horizontally abducting the arms while keeping the torso hinged. Performed with a slight forward lean and controlled scapular retraction to emphasize posterior shoulder muscles.

exercises.name: Cable One Arm Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on the low pulley and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on the low pulley and select an appropriate weight.
Stand upright next to the machine with the working side closest to the pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and core braced.
Grasp the handle with a neutral grip (thumb slightly forward) and allow the arm to hang down at your side with a small bend in the elbow.
Keep your torso stable (no rotation) and, if needed, take a small step away from the machine to create tension in the cable.
Exhale and raise the arm out to the side in a controlled arc until the hand reaches about shoulder height (elbow stays slightly bent); avoid shrugging the shoulder.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulder engaged, then inhale and slowly lower the arm back to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps on one arm, then switch sides. Maintain steady tempo and avoid using momentum or leaning the torso to lift the weight.
exercises.description: A single-arm lateral raise performed with a low cable to target the lateral head of the deltoid; the cable provides continuous tension through the movement. Performed standing, the arm is lifted out to the side to about shoulder height with a slight elbow bend.

exercises.name: Cable Front Raise (rope attachment)
exercises.notes: Set the cable to the lowest position and attach a rope.
instructions: Set the cable to the lowest position and attach a rope. Stand facing away from the weight stack (facing forward) with the rope between your legs.

Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced. Grasp the rope ends with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) holding the rope in front of your thighs; maintain a slight bend in the elbows.

Keep your torso stationary and shoulders down; exhale and lift the rope straight forward and up by flexing at the shoulder until your forearms are roughly parallel to the floor (shoulder height). Lead the movement with the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top and focus on contracting the front of the delts; keep wrists neutral and avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and lower the rope slowly and under control back to the starting position, maintaining the slight elbow bend and core bracing.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions. Use a weight that allows controlled motion—avoid swinging or using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable front raises with a rope attachment target the anterior deltoids by lifting the rope straight in front of the body from a low pulley; the movement uses a controlled shoulder flexion with a slight elbow bend. Core and grip muscles assist to stabilize the torso and handle the load.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Face Pull (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set a rope handle on a high pulley (about head/eye level) and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a rope handle on a high pulley (about head/eye level) and select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright facing the machine with feet flat on the floor and knees bent (or seated on a bench); keep a tall spine and neutral head position.
Grasp the rope with a neutral grip (thumbs pointing toward you) and extend your arms so there is tension on the cable.
Initiate the movement by pulling the rope toward your face, leading with your elbows and keeping them at about shoulder height.
As you pull, externally rotate your shoulders so the ends of the rope separate and your hands move either side of your face; squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end range.
Pause briefly at peak contraction, keeping the torso stable and avoiding excessive backward lean or shrugging.
Slowly return the rope to the start position with control, maintaining tension in the cable and scapular control throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of reps and maintain controlled tempo to protect the shoulder joint.
exercises.description: A seated cable face pull using a rope attachment that emphasizes the rear delts and upper posterior shoulder musculature by pulling the rope toward the face while externally rotating the hands and squeezing the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing inward), arms fully relaxed.
Brace your core and press your lower back lightly into the bench to maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Slightly bend your elbows (soft arms) and keep the bend constant for the entire movement.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a controlled arc until your arms are roughly parallel to the floor (shoulder height); lead with the elbows and keep wrists neutral.
Keep shoulders down (do not shrug) and avoid leaning or using torso momentum—if you need to swing, reduce the weight.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position.
Perform repetitions with a steady tempo (e.g., 1–2 seconds up, 1–2 seconds down) and choose a weight that allows strict form.
exercises.description: A seated lateral raise isolates the lateral head of the deltoid by lifting light-to-moderate dumbbells out to the sides from a seated, supported position. Use a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to emphasize shoulder abduction and limit momentum.

exercises.name: Cable Y raise
exercises.notes: Set two low pulleys (near the floor) and attach single D-handles; select a light–moderate weight.
instructions: Set two low pulleys (near the floor) and attach single D-handles; select a light–moderate weight.
Stand between or slightly in front of the pulleys with feet hip-width apart, core braced and a slight bend in the knees.
Grasp the handles with a neutral grip (thumbs pointing up) so the cables cross in front of your body, arms hanging down.
Begin the movement by raising your arms diagonally and outward in a Y shape (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) with a small bend in the elbows.
Continue lifting until your hands are roughly overhead or slightly in front of you, keeping the shoulders down and scapulae rotating upward (avoid excessive shrugging).
Pause briefly at the top to feel the delts and scapular stabilizers engage, then lower the handles slowly and under control back to the start.
Maintain a stable torso throughout—avoid leaning back or using momentum; use a lighter weight if you must swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: A standing cable variation where you pull the handles upward and outward in a Y-shaped path to emphasize the shoulder delts and scapular upward rotation. It trains the delts while engaging the traps and serratus anterior to stabilize and rotate the shoulder blades.
